content:
May 29, 2026 [compiler-design] #memory-compiler #sram #eda #layout-vs-schematic #tiling
Most people see the name "memory compiler" and have no idea what it actually does.

1. Compiler Representation

The classical representation is the T-diagram (or Tombstone diagram). It characterizes a compiler by three languages: the source language it reads (A), the target language it emits (B), and the implementation language it is written in (C):

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ A вҶ’ B в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ӯ C
Read it as: "a program that translates A into B, written in C."

The textbook example is C вҶ’ machine code. But the definition is broader than that:

вҖў Typical compiler: C / C++ вҶ’ assembly / machine code

вҖў The compiler I work on at Synopsys: VHDL / Verilog RTL вҶ’ simulation / debugging database

вҖў Memory compiler: design parameters (depth, width, portвҖҰ) вҶ’ all views needed to tape out a chip

вҖў Shader compiler: GLSL / HLSL вҶ’ GPU machine code (e.g., Mesa's NIR pipeline, DXC)

вҖў Query compiler: SQL вҶ’ a physical execution plan (e.g., PostgreSQL's planner/executor)

вҖў Bytecode compiler: Java source вҶ’ JVM bytecode (javac), or JavaScript вҶ’ V8 bytecode (Ignition)

2. The Raw Material: Inside a Bitcell

The fundamental building block of any SRAM (Static Random-Access Memory) is the bitcell вҖ” a tiny circuit that holds one bit. Hardware teams design and characterize these cells by hand; the memory compiler's job is to replicate them at scale.

The most common variant is the 6T bitcell (six transistors). Three signal lines connect it to the outside world: WL (Word Line вҖ” selects a row), BL (Bit Line), and BLB (Bit Line Bar, the complementary signal):

WL
в•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Ұв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Ұв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җ
в•‘ в•‘
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв•«в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв•«в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ [ M5 ] [ M6 ] в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
BL в”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв—Ҹв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Q Q' в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв—Ҹв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җ BLB
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв–әв”Җв”Ө INV_R в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ(M2,M4)в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө INV_L в”ңв”Җв—„в”Җв”Ө в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ(M1,M3)в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ bitcell (6T) в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Transistor count:

вҖў INV_L: PMOS M3 + NMOS M1 = 2

вҖў INV_R: PMOS M4 + NMOS M2 = 2

вҖў Access transistors: M5, M6 = 2

вҖў Total: 6 вҶ’ "6T"

Positive Feedback: How a Bit Gets Locked

Two inverters connected head-to-tail (cross-coupled):

вҖў Assume Q = 1 (high, Vdd)

вҖў INV_R receives 1, outputs 0 to Q'

вҖў INV_L receives 0, outputs 1 back to Q

вҖў Q stays 1 вҖ” a perfect closed loop

This is positive feedback + bistability. As long as Vdd is present, Q and Q' are locked at opposite voltages indefinitely. The stored bit is literally just the voltage sitting on those two nodes. SRAM is called "Static" because вҖ” unlike DRAM вҖ” it never needs to refresh.

Three Operations

Hold (Standby)

вҖў WL = 0, access transistors off

вҖў Cross-coupled inverters maintain state entirely on their own; only leakage current flows

вҖў Strictly speaking, Hold is a state, not an operation вҖ” but datasheets list it alongside Read/Write because designers need leakage current figures for power budgeting

Read

вҖў Precharge BL and BLB to Vdd

вҖў Assert WL high; M5 and M6 turn on

вҖў The side storing 0 pulls its bitline down by ~100 mV

вҖў Sense amplifier amplifies the small differential вҶ’ 1 bit out

Read Disturbance: the moment WL turns on, the high-voltage BLB can pull up Q' (the node storing 0) slightly through M6. If it rises past the switching threshold of INV_L, the cell flips вҖ” a Destructive Read. The fix is making the pull-down NMOS M2 stronger (wider) than the access transistor M6. This strength ratio is the Beta Ratio (ОІ = W_pull-down / W_access), typically required to be > 1. This is why the six transistors in a standard bitcell are not all the same size.

Write

вҖў Write driver forces BL and BLB to target values (one high, one low)

вҖў Assert WL high

вҖў Write driver strength overcomes the cell's pull-up вҶ’ forces the cell to flip

вҖў The sizing trade-off between these forces is called the pull-up ratio

Beyond 6T: Other Bitcell Types

6T is not the only option. Different use cases demand different trade-offs:

CellTransistorsKey propertyTypical use

6T6High density, standard read/writeL2/L3 caches

8T8Isolated read port, no read disturbL1 caches, register files

10T10Ultra-low voltage operationNear-threshold designs

The 8T cell adds a dedicated read path (two extra transistors) so the read operation never touches the storage nodes вҖ” eliminating read disturbance entirely at the cost of ~33% more area.

3. Why a Memory Compiler Exists

When a hardware team designs an SoC (System on Chip), they need SRAM вҖ” lots of it, at many different sizes. The fundamental unit they work with is a cell: the smallest verified building block of an SRAM array. But a chip might need an SRAM of depth 512 Г— width 32 in one place, and depth 4096 Г— width 64 in another. Redesigning a new cell from scratch for every configuration is not feasible.

The solution is a parameterized IP: instead of a fixed design, you ship a tool that accepts parameters and generates the correct implementation automatically. That tool is the memory compiler. Given depth, width, port count, and a few other knobs, it produces a complete, tapeout-ready SRAM macro.

A memory compiler generates a complete, self-contained SRAM macro вҖ” the bitcell array plus all the peripheral circuitry needed to operate it: row/column decoders, sense amplifiers, write drivers, and self-timed control. What it does not generate is the memory controller вҖ” the system-level logic that decides what to read and write, handles arbitration and BIST sequencing, and lives in the SoC RTL outside the macro.

Leaf cell views в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ Generated View
(from hardware team): в”Ӯ Memory Compiler в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
.gds / .oas (layout) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ>в”Ӯ в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җ> .gds / .oas
.lef (abstract) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ>в”Ӯ в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җ> .lef
.cdl / .sp (netlist) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ>в”Ӯ в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җ> .lib
.lib (timing) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ>в”Ӯ в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җ> .v (Verilog)
в”Ӯ в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җ> .sp / .cdl
Parameters: в”Ӯ в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җ> .cpf / .upf
в”Ӯ в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җ> .pat (ATPG/MBIST)
depth в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ>в”Ӯ в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җ> .pdf (Datasheet)
width в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ>в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
port в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ>в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
mux factor в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ>в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
corner (PVT) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ>в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳOutput Views

ViewFormatUsed by

LayoutGDS / OASISTapeout, sent to fab

AbstractLEFP&R (Place and Route) вҖ” exposes pins + blockage only

Timing.lib (Liberty)STA (Static Timing Analysis) вҖ” setup/hold, access time

BehavioralVerilog / SVRTL simulation

NetlistSPICE / CDLSPICE simulation, LVS

Power.lib / CPF / UPFPower analysis

TestATPG / MBIST patternDFT (Design for Testability) / manufacturing test

4. Two Teams, One Boundary

A memory compiler sits at the intersection of two teams with very different jobs.

The Hardware Team: Designing the Leaf Cell

The hardware team вҖ” circuit designers and layout engineers вҖ” designs and verifies the leaf cell: the smallest possible SRAM unit. Designing a single 6T bitcell involves resolving multiple interacting constraints:

вҖў Transistor sizing (Beta ratio, pull-up ratio) to balance read stability vs. write ability

вҖў Full-custom layout at the process node's design rules

вҖў Characterization across all PVT corners (dozens of SPICE simulations)

вҖў Sign-off: DRC (Design Rule Check), LVS (Layout vs Schematic), antenna checks, electromigration

The hardware team delivers a small set of verified, hand-crafted files вҖ” GDS, CDL, LEF, .lib вҖ” for that one leaf cell. Their job ends at the single-cell boundary.

The CAD Team: Building the Compiler

The CAD team (or memory compiler team) takes those verified leaf cells and builds the automation that scales them to any size. Their job is to answer: given a leaf cell that works correctly in isolation, how do you tile it into an array of arbitrary depth Г— width while guaranteeing:

вҖў LVS passes вҖ” GDS and netlist tiling must match exactly

вҖў DRC passes вҖ” abutment boundaries must be clean at every process node's design rules

вҖў All output views are consistent вҖ” the .lib timing model, the Verilog behavioral model, the SPICE netlist, and the GDS all describe the same circuit

Why Not Just Write a Script?

A script-based flow (e.g., using Tcl or Python) is a common initial approach for structural assembly. While functional for simple cases, it introduces specific limitations as complexity scales:

ProblemWhy a script struggles

Multiple output formatsEach format has different tiling logic; keeping them in sync manually is error-prone

LVS correctnessA single off-by-one in row numbering silently produces an LVS mismatch across thousands of nets

New configurationsAdding a new mux ratio or port combination requires touching tiling logic in every format independently

Regression safetyNo shared data model means a fix in GDS tiling doesn't automatically propagate to netlist tiling

Bitcell swapChanging the leaf cell requires hunting down every hardcoded assumption across every format script

The last point deserves emphasis. In a script-based flow, the bitcell's geometry and netlist structure leak into the tiling logic вҖ” net names, transistor counts, pin locations get hardcoded. Swap a 6T cell for an 8T cell, and the script breaks in multiple places simultaneously, across multiple files, in ways that may not be immediately obvious.

A memory compiler treats the bitcell views (GDS, CDL, .lib) as inputs, not assumptions baked into the code. The tiling engine doesn't know or care what's inside the cell вҖ” it only knows how to replicate and connect it. Swap the input files, and every output format updates automatically. Consistency is structural, not manual.

A memory compiler solves this by having one data structure drive all emitters. The tiling logic runs once; GDS, CDL, Verilog, and .lib are all derived outputs. This is the same architectural insight behind any compiler: separate the representation of intent from the multiple backends that render it.

5. The Parameter Space

depth Г— width вҖ” Array Shape

<в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ width = 32 bits в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ>
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җ ... в”Җв”Җв”җ вҶ‘
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җ ... в”Җв”Җв”Ө в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ depth = 1024 words
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җ ... в”Җв”Җв”Ө в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җ ... в”Җв”Җв”ҳ вҶ“
Total bits = 1024 Г— 32 = 32,768 bitsport вҖ” Independent Access Channels

Feature1-Port (1P / 1RW)Multi-Port (e.g., 2R1W)

Address buses12+ (separate read addr, write addr)

ConcurrencyRead or WriteRead and Write simultaneously

BitcellStandard 6T8T (isolated read port)

AreaHigh density~1.5xвҖ“2.0x penalty

ApplicationsL1/L2/L3 cachesCPU register files, FIFOs

Register files require multi-port arrays to prevent pipeline stalls. Executing ADD R1, R2, R3 requires reading R2 and R3 simultaneously while writing back a previous instruction's result into R1. A 1-port SRAM forces serial execution, breaking pipeline concurrency. Consequently, register files absorb the 8T area penalty to sustain throughput.

mux factor вҖ” Column Multiplexer Ratio

Sense amplifiers are expensive in area. The mux factor decides how many bitline columns share one sense amp:

mux=1 (no mux): mux=4:
one SA per column one SA per 4 columns
в”Ңв”Җв”¬в”Җв”¬в”Җв”¬в”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”¬в”Җв”¬в”Җв”¬в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
SA SA SA SA в””в”Җв”ҙв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”ҳ
SA
fastest, widest layout slower, narrower layout

// SA = Sense Amplifier: detects the small voltage differential
// on BL/BLB and amplifies it to a full logic level
An easy-to-miss point: mux factor doesn't only affect area вҖ” it directly affects latency. Every cycle spent selecting which column connects to the sense amp is latency. Higher mux factor means longer bitlines, more capacitance, and a slower sense amp enable. A significant portion of cache access latency is hiding inside the column mux вҖ” this is a first-class trade-off knob in L1 microarchitecture design, not just a layout convenience.

corner вҖ” PVT (Process, Voltage, Temperature)

Process: TT (typical), SS (slow), FF (fast)
Voltage: 0.72V (low), 0.8V (nom), 0.88V (high)
Temp: -40В°C, 25В°C, 125В°C

SS + high temp + low voltage вҶ’ worst-case timing (setup check)
FF + low temp + high voltage вҶ’ best-case timing (hold check)
The .lib file contains one timing
